questions.q: What is the range of wavelengths that the human eye can perceive as visible light?
Which three colors are the primary colors in the RGB color model used by screens and displays?
What is the term for the color-mixing method used by televisions and computer monitors to create colors?
Which color model uses cyan, magenta, yellow, and black as its primary components?
What pigment is responsible for giving human eyes their brown color?
How many types of cone cells does the human eye typically contain?
What is the optical phenomenon where you see a color's opposite after staring at it and then looking away?
Which three colors appear on the French flag?
What is the theory of color vision that explains color perception through opposing color pairs?
Which three colors are featured on the German flag?
What type of color is perceived when an object reflects all wavelengths of light equally?
What condition affects an individual's ability to distinguish between certain colors in the visible spectrum?
Which type of cone cells in the human eye are most sensitive to blue light?
What is the term for the alternative healing practice that uses colors to promote well-being and health?
What scientific principle states that the human eye perceives color based on stimulation of three cone types?
In the visible spectrum, which color has the shortest wavelength that humans can perceive?
What is the term for colors that are produced by mixing light rather than pigments?
Which color is generally considered the most visible to the human eye in daylight conditions?
What is the term for colors that appear on the traditional artist's color wheel, such as red, blue, and yellow?
What do we call two colors that sit directly opposite each other on the color wheel?
Which type of cells in the human eye are primarily responsible for seeing colors in bright light?
Which gemstone's name is also used to describe a blue-green color found on the color wheel?
What term describes how pure or intense a color appears, ranging from dull to vivid?
In printing, what color mixing method is used to create images using inks on paper?
What happens when you mix all three primary colors of light together at full intensity?
Which color typically appears after red in the traditional rainbow sequence?
What is the name for colors created by mixing a primary color with a secondary color?
In the RGB color model, what does the letter 'G' stand for?
What color is produced when blue light and red light are combined additively?
Which color is traditionally associated with the warning or danger in most cultures?
What do we call a color that has been lightened by adding white to it?
In the CMYK color model used in printing, what does the 'K' stand for?
Which secondary color is created by mixing red and blue pigments together?
Which color is traditionally associated with mourning and funerals in many East Asian cultures?
Which atmospheric phenomenon causes the sky to appear blue on a clear day?
What are the three primary colors traditionally shown on the artist's color wheel?
What is the most common type of color blindness affecting the ability to distinguish red and green?
Which color model is used by televisions and computer screens to produce colors by mixing light?
What term describes colors that combine to appear white when mixed as light?
Which two colors are commonly combined in fast-food restaurant branding to stimulate appetite?
What scientific principle explains why peacock feathers and butterfly wings display vibrant colors without pigments?
In the 1800s, what dangerous chemical pigment did painters use to create a bright yellow color in artworks?
What is the term for colors that appear across the entire visible spectrum in pure, undiluted form?
In what year did scientist Thomas Young propose the trichromatic theory of color vision?
What color is produced when you mix cyan, magenta, and yellow inks in printing?
Which cells in the human eye are primarily responsible for vision in low-light conditions rather than color perception?
Which color has the longest wavelength in the visible light spectrum?
According to international surveys, which color is most commonly cited as the world's favorite?
Which secondary color is created by mixing red and yellow together?
Short-wavelength cones in the human eye are most responsive to which color of light?
What is the phenomenon where objects appear to maintain consistent color despite changing lighting conditions?

In 1666, Isaac Newton used a prism to demonstrate that white light could be separated into a spectrum. Which color did he identify as appearing twice in his original seven-color spectrum classification?
The CIE 1931 color space standard introduced the concept of which mathematical function to represent how the human eye responds to different wavelengths of light?
Structural color in peacock feathers and butterfly wings is primarily created by which physical mechanism rather than pigmentation?
In the opponent-process color theory, which neural structure in the brain is responsible for processing color information through opposing color channels?
Tetrachromacy in humans, where individuals possess four types of cone cells, is theoretically most common among which demographic group?
choices: Women with a specific genetic variation

questions.q: The MacAdam ellipses, introduced in 1942, demonstrate which fundamental principle about human color perception?
choices: Not all color differences are equally perceptible across the color space
Color blindness follows a predictable genetic pattern
Additive color mixing always produces more vibrant results
Saturation levels remain constant regardless of luminance

questions.q: When combining cyan, magenta, and yellow inks in printing without black ink, what color typically results from overlapping all three at full saturation?
The Munsell Color System, developed in 1905, organized colors according to three independent perceptual dimensions. Which of these is NOT one of them?
In the context of color-blind individuals, which type of color vision deficiency affects approximately 0.001% of the population and involves the absence of all color discrimination?
The phenomenon of color constancy allows us to perceive an object's color as stable despite changes in illumination. Which brain area is primarily responsible for this perceptual mechanism?
choices: Ventral visual stream in the temporal cortex
Dorsal visual stream in the parietal cortex

questions.q: When light is additively mixed using equal intensities of red, green, and blue at their peak wavelengths, the resulting perception is not perfectly white for most observers. What is this discrepancy called?
The International Color Consortium (ICC) profile standard uses which color space as its platform-independent reference for color management?
In human color vision, which cone cell type has the lowest absolute sensitivity but the sharpest wavelength discrimination ability?
choices: All cone types have equal discrimination

questions.q: In the HSL (Hue, Saturation, Lightness) color model, what does a saturation value of 0% represent regardless of the hue value?
choices: The primary color at full intensity

questions.q: The gamut of colors that can be reproduced by a specific device is mathematically defined within which type of three-dimensional geometric structure?

Which mathematical principle explains why certain pairs of colors, when additively mixed in specific proportions, produce the sensation of white light despite containing no wavelengths that correspond to white?
The Helmholtz-Kohlrausch effect describes the phenomenon where colors of equal luminance appear to have different brightness levels based on which property?
In colorimetry, which type of observer was standardized in 1931 and uses a 2-degree visual field for measuring color?
The YUV color model, commonly used in video compression and broadcasting, separates color information into which components?
choices: Cyan, magenta, yellow, and black components

questions.q: In the opponent process theory of color vision, which pair of colors cannot be perceived together as a single hue due to the way retinal ganglion cells are organized?
What is the primary physiological mechanism responsible for the sky appearing blue during daytime, as opposed to violet which is also scattered by the same process?
choices: Rayleigh scattering combined with the sun's peak emission and eye sensitivity
Diffraction of light through atmospheric dust particles

questions.q: Which color model, based on the LMS cone response model, is most directly aligned with how the human visual system processes color at the photoreceptor level?
Individuals with tetrachromacy possess four types of color receptors instead of three; in which sex is this condition almost exclusively observed?
In the context of color-opponent processing, the lateral geniculate nucleus receives input from retinal ganglion cells and organizes color information into which fundamental opponent channels?
What is the mathematical boundary within three-dimensional color space that defines the limits of colors perceivable by the human eye, also known as the optimal color solid?
When subtractive color mixing is used in CMYK printing, what optical phenomenon explains why cyan, magenta, and yellow inks do not produce a true black?
choices: Spectral incompleteness and imperfect pigment absorption
Insufficient ink saturation on the substrate
Rayleigh scattering at the ink surface

questions.q: Which cone cell type in the human retina has peak sensitivity around 555 nanometers under photopic conditions and dominates luminance perception?
Protanopia and deuteranopia are both forms of red-green color blindness; which genetic mutation pattern distinguishes protanopia from deuteranopia?
choices: Protanopia involves absence of L cones; deuteranopia involves absence of M cones
Protanopia is autosomal dominant; deuteranopia is X-linked recessive
Protanopia affects rod cells; deuteranopia affects cone cells
Protanopia causes complete achromatopsia; deuteranopia causes partial color blindness

questions.q: In additive color mixing used in display technology, which primary colors are selected specifically because they align with the peak sensitivities of the three cone types?
The MacAdam ellipses in color space represent regions of color indistinguishability to the human eye; why do these ellipses vary in size across different regions of the chromaticity diagram?
choices: Due to non-uniform distribution of cone cell types across the retina
Because color discrimination ability is non-uniform in perceptual color space
Because different wavelengths scatter light differently
Due to lens aberrations that vary by color

questions.q: What artistic technique did Vincent van Gogh employ by placing complementary colors adjacent to each other, exploiting the optical properties of color contrast?
In the CIE 1931 color space, why is the chromaticity diagram shaped irregularly rather than being a perfect circle or square?
choices: To account for non-uniform color discrimination across wavelengths
Because the human eye perceives color in cylindrical coordinates
Due to the mathematical transformation from tristimulus values to chromaticity
To match the spectral sensitivity of rod cells

questions.q: Approximately what percentage of the male population exhibits red-green color blindness, and which X-linked gene mutation is the primary cause?
choices: 3-4%, mutations in opsin genes for M or L cones
15%, mutations in retinal pigment epithelium genes

questions.q: Why cannot modern RGB displays or CMYK printers reproduce all colors within the optimal color solid, despite being the standard for color reproduction?
choices: The gamut of these systems is inherently smaller than the full range of human color perception
Display refresh rates are too slow to show all colors simultaneously
Metamerism prevents exact color matching between systems
Cone cell sensitivity varies too much between individuals

questions.q: In color-blindness testing using Ishihara plates, what principle of color contrast allows individuals with normal color vision to perceive numbers that are invisible to red-green color-blind observers?
choices: Simultaneous color contrast and differential hue discrimination
Luminance differences combined with saturation masking
Chromatic aberration at the edge of perception

questions.q: Which three primary colors form the basis of the traditional RYB (Red-Yellow-Blue) color model taught in art education, despite being scientifically different from the RGB additive primaries?
choices: They align with historical pigment availability and intuitive mixing results
They correspond to the three cone cell types in the human eye
They are based on subtractive color mixing principles, unlike RGB
They represent the wavelengths most sensitive to rod cells

questions.q: The CIELAB color space was designed to be more perceptually uniform than the CIE 1931 XYZ space; what mathematical transformation accomplishes this non-linearity?
choices: Logarithmic compression of each tristimulus value component
Cube root transformation of tristimulus values relative to a reference white
Polar coordinate conversion with saturation weighting

questions.q: In the CIE 1931 color space, what does the 'xy' chromaticity diagram specifically exclude when representing color?
The opponent-process theory of color vision proposes that color information is processed through opposing pairs at which neural structure?
What is the maximum theoretical color gamut that the sRGB color space can reproduce relative to the human-visible spectrum?
In the LAB color model, what does the 'L' axis specifically represent independent of other color dimensions?
choices: Lightness independent of hue and saturation

questions.q: The visible light spectrum spans approximately 390 to 700 nanometers. Which color occupies the narrowest range of wavelengths in this spectrum?
The Ishihara color blindness test uses what underlying principle to differentiate color-blind from normal color vision?
choices: Red-green hue discrimination through embedded patterns

questions.q: Deuteranopia, a form of red-green color blindness, results from the absence or dysfunction of which cone photopigment?
In the HSL (Hue, Saturation, Lightness) color model, what value of saturation corresponds to a completely desaturated, achromatic color?
The Bezold-Brücke effect describes how perceived hue shifts when which color property is increased?
ICC color profiles enable color management by creating a mapping between device-dependent color spaces and which device-independent reference space?
The MacLeod-Boynton chromaticity diagram was developed to better represent which aspect of human color vision?
choices: Cone cell distribution across the retina

questions.q: In the Munsell color system, which dimension uses a nonlinear scale specifically designed to match human perception of equal color differences?
choices: All three dimensions use nonlinear perceptual scales

questions.q: The photopic luminosity function peaks at approximately 555 nanometers under daylight conditions. Under scotopic (low-light) conditions, this peak shifts to approximately which wavelength?
Cerebral achromatopsia, a rare neurological condition affecting color perception, results from damage to which brain region?
choices: Human color form area (V4) and surrounding regions

questions.q: The CIECAM02 color appearance model was developed to account for color appearance under varying conditions. What key factor does it incorporate that simpler models neglect?
In the retinex theory of color constancy proposed by Edwin Land, how does the visual system estimate true surface color despite changing illumination?
choices: By averaging wavelengths across the entire visual field
By computing ratios of light intensity at boundaries between areas
By filtering out all wavelengths below 500 nanometers
By using only the long-wavelength cone signals

questions.q: In the Munsell color system, which dimension measures the degree to which a color differs from a neutral gray of the same value?
What is the name of the phenomenon where the eye's sensitivity to color decreases under very low light conditions, causing red wavelengths to appear darker than blue wavelengths?
Which specific type of cone cell dysfunction is responsible for tritanopia, a rare form of color blindness?
What is the scientific term for the color sensation produced when the eye is exposed to wavelengths between approximately 380 and 450 nanometers?
In the LAB color space, what does the 'a' axis represent?
In human vision, which photopigment is primarily responsible for detecting long-wavelength light in L-type cone cells?
What is the primary weakness of the CIE 1931 standard observer model when applied to modern color reproduction technology?
choices: It uses a narrow 2-degree field of view that doesn't account for peripheral vision
It cannot accurately represent colors in the blue spectrum
It was designed for tungsten light only
It overestimates the sensitivity of rod cells

questions.q: In the HSL color model, what does the 'L' component specifically measure?
choices: Lightness, ranging from black through the pure color to white
Luminance relative to human perceptual sensitivity
The long-wavelength component of the color
Linear intensity on a logarithmic scale

questions.q: What is the primary advantage of using the CMYK color model in commercial printing?
choices: It uses additive color mixing like screens
It employs subtractive mixing to produce colors on reflective surfaces
It requires fewer inks than RGB
It eliminates the need for color management

questions.q: In the opponent process theory of color vision, what are the three primary color channels?
Which color model uses hue, saturation, and lightness as its three primary dimensions?
Which trichromatic color vision theory was proposed in the early 19th century to explain how humans perceive color?
In color reproduction systems, what term describes colors that have maximum purity and are fully saturated?
What is the purpose of ICC profiles in digital color management?
choices: To map input colors to device gamut and ensure consistent color reproduction
To eliminate the need for color calibration
To replace the RGB color model

questions.q: Which color model is specifically designed for television and video transmission and uses luminance and chrominance channels?
What is the term for the descriptive names of colors derived from natural objects, such as 'sky blue' or 'rose red'?
In additive color mixing, what color is produced when red and green light are combined at full intensity?
In color theory, what term describes the complete set of colors that a display or printing device can reproduce?
Which psychological effect describes the tendency for colors to be perceived differently depending on surrounding colors?
What physiological response has been scientifically linked to the color red?
In black-and-white photography, why does using a red filter cause red objects to appear lighter?
choices: Red filters absorb all other colors of light
Red filters enhance red wavelengths, allowing more light reflection
Red filters increase the film's sensitivity

questions.q: The Himba people of Namibia are noted in linguistic research for what specific color perception difficulty?
choices: Unable to distinguish red from orange
Unable to differentiate blue from green
Unable to distinguish red from purple

questions.q: Which color model represents colors using cylindrical coordinates including hue, saturation, and value?
What is the complementary color to blue in traditional color theory?
In subtractive color mixing, what color results from combining cyan and magenta inks at full saturation?
What does the CIE chromaticity diagram primarily serve to represent?
choices: The gamut and color space of reproduction systems
The wavelength distribution of specific objects
The perception speed of the human eye

questions.q: What is saturation in color reproduction, and how does it differ from hue?
choices: Saturation is brightness; hue is the color itself
Saturation is the intensity or purity of a color; hue is the color identity
Saturation is wavelength; hue is frequency
Saturation is darkness; hue is lightness

questions.q: Which condition results from neural damage in the brain that causes complete inability to perceive any colors?
Which type of cone cell in the human eye is most sensitive to light in the red portion of the visible spectrum?
What phenomenon allows two physically different light combinations to appear identical to the human eye?
In the visible light spectrum, approximately what wavelength range does green light occupy?
What is the condition called when a person cannot distinguish between red and green colors due to missing or deficient cone types?
In color management, what is the primary purpose of using a color profile in digital imaging workflows?
choices: To ensure consistent color reproduction across different devices
To reduce the number of colors in an image
To create a vintage filter effect

questions.q: Which cone cell type in the human eye provides the most detailed information about color discrimination in daylight conditions?
choices: A combination of all three cone types

questions.q: What happens physically when white light passes through a cyan-colored filter?
choices: Red light is absorbed while cyan light is transmitted
Cyan light is absorbed while red light is transmitted
Green and blue light are transmitted while red is absorbed

questions.q: In digital color representation, how many bits are typically used to encode each color channel in a standard 24-bit RGB image?
In the opponent process theory of color vision, which pair of colors is NOT one of the three fundamental opponent channels?
In subtractive color mixing, which three pigment colors are primarily used in traditional printing?
Which international organization established the CIE chromaticity diagram in 1931 to standardize color measurement?
Approximately what percentage of the male population experiences red-green color blindness?
What three primary colors are used in additive color (light) on screens?

source.notes: RGB. Used in screens and digital displays.

questions.q: What are the four ink colors used in CMYK color printing?

source.notes: K stands for 'key' (black) — kept separate to avoid printing-registration issues.

questions.q: What does the mnemonic ROYGBIV represent?

source.notes: Red, Orange, Yellow, Green, Blue, Indigo, Violet.

questions.q: Mixing red and white paint produces what color?
Which secondary color is made by mixing blue and yellow?
Which pigment gives skin, hair, and eyes their color?
Which luxury jewelry company is associated with a distinctive robin's-egg blue?

source.notes: Tiffany Blue — trademarked 1998. Pantone-matched #1837 (Tiffany's founding year).

questions.q: Which French artist patented his own ultramarine-blue pigment, called International ___ Blue?

source.notes: Yves Klein patented IKB (International Klein Blue) in 1960.

questions.q: In which culture is white often associated with mourning rather than weddings?
Which color was famously absent from M&M packs from 1976 to 1987 due to a Red Dye scare?

source.notes: Mars pulled red M&Ms in response to the Red Dye No. 2 controversy — even though M&Ms used a different dye. Returned in 1987.

questions.q: Which 2015 internet phenomenon split the internet over whether a striped dress was 'blue and black' or 'white and gold'?

source.notes: Originally photographed at a wedding in Scotland; the dress was actually blue and black.

questions.q: In a black-and-white film/photograph, which color filter darkens blue skies for dramatic contrast?

source.original_a: Red filters enhance red tones, making the skin reflect more light.
source.notes: Red blocks blue/cyan light, so the sky appears very dark.
